---
title: "Washington residential customers — share of customers, energy, and peak load by heating group"
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

This is a back-of-envelope analysis comparing Washington residential customers by their heating type (heat pump, electric resistance, natural gas, etc.) on three dimensions: how many of them there are, how much electricity they consume in a year, and how much load they contribute during the top 100 system-load hours.

The first two are proxies for **share of delivery revenue** under a flat volumetric rate; the third is a proxy for **share of demand-driven cost of service** (an embedded-cost-of-service-style demand allocator). The gap between revenue share and cost share is a rough indicator of where cross-subsidies are likely happening — without needing dollars to see it.

This notebook lives in `reports/ny_hp_rates/notebooks/` so we don't have to spin up a whole new Quarto project for one table. It's not part of the published `ny_hp_rates` report and is not in the `_quarto.yml` render list.

## Setup

```{python}
#| label: setup

from __future__ import annotations

from pathlib import Path

import polars as pl
from great_tables import GT, loc, style

from lib.great_tables import get_switchbox_gt_tab_options
from lib.quarto import display_gt
```

## Parameters

```{python}
#| label: params

# Both paths are local on the EBS volume; ResStock loads from S3 take 28+ min for a whole state,
# whereas scanning ~12k local parquets is on the order of seconds.
PATH_METADATA = Path(
    "/ebs/data/nrel/resstock/res_2024_amy2018_2_sb"
    "/metadata/state=WA/upgrade=00/metadata-sb.parquet"
)
PATH_HOURLY_DIR = Path(
    "/ebs/data/nrel/resstock/res_2024_amy2018_2"
    "/load_curve_hourly/state=WA/upgrade=00"
)

TOP_N_HOURS = 100
```

We use the same heating-group ordering and labels as the NY cost-of-service tables so cross-state comparison is easy:

```{python}
#| label: heating-groups

HT_V2_ORDER = (
    "heat_pump",
    "electrical_resistance",
    "natgas",
    "delivered_fuels",
    "other",
)
HT_V2_LABELS: dict[str, str] = {
    "heat_pump": "Heat pump",
    "electrical_resistance": "Electric resistance",
    "natgas": "Natural gas",
    "delivered_fuels": "Delivered fuels",
    "other": "Other",
}
```

## Load metadata

Each row in this metadata file represents one ResStock building (a single-family home or a unit in a multifamily building) in Washington under the baseline (upgrade 00). The columns we care about are `bldg_id`, `weight` (the CAIRO sample weight — number of real-world households this row represents), and `postprocess_group.heating_type_v2` (the Switchbox-derived heating-group label, added by `data/resstock/identify_heating_type.py` in `rate-design-platform`).

```{python}
#| label: load-metadata

md = (
    pl.read_parquet(PATH_METADATA)
    .select(
        "bldg_id",
        "weight",
        pl.col("postprocess_group.heating_type_v2").alias("subclass"),
    )
)
print(f"Buildings: {md.height:,}  |  Weighted households: {md['weight'].sum():,.0f}")
md.head(5)
```

Let's count buildings and weighted households per heating group as a sanity check:

```{python}
#| label: subclass-counts

md_by_sub = (
    md.group_by("subclass")
    .agg(
        pl.len().alias("n_buildings"),
        pl.col("weight").sum().alias("n_customers"),
    )
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("n_customers") / pl.col("n_customers").sum()).alias("pct_customers"),
    )
    .sort("n_customers", descending=True)
)
md_by_sub
```

## Build hourly load × heating group

We want one row per `(timestamp, subclass)` with the **statewide weighted residential load** in kWh — collapsing 12,246 buildings × 8,760 hours down to ~5 × 8,760 = 43,800 rows. Polars can stream this in one lazy pipeline: scan all hourly parquets, attach `bldg_id` from the file path, join to metadata to bring in `weight` and `subclass`, multiply load by weight, and group by `(timestamp, subclass)`. Only the 43,800-row aggregate is materialized.

```{python}
#| label: hourly-by-subclass

md_lf = md.lazy()

hourly_by_sub = (
    pl.scan_parquet(str(PATH_HOURLY_DIR / "*.parquet"), include_file_paths="path")
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("path")
        .str.extract(r"(\d+)-0\.parquet$")
        .cast(pl.Int64)
        .alias("bldg_id"),
    )
    .select(
        "bldg_id",
        "timestamp",
        pl.col("out.electricity.total.energy_consumption").alias("kwh"),
    )
    .join(md_lf, on="bldg_id", how="inner")
    .with_columns((pl.col("kwh") * pl.col("weight")).alias("kwh_w"))
    .group_by(["timestamp", "subclass"])
    .agg(pl.sum("kwh_w").alias("kwh_w_state"))
    .collect()
)
print(f"hourly_by_sub: {hourly_by_sub.shape}  (timestamps × subclasses)")
hourly_by_sub.head(5)
```

Quick sanity check on magnitudes — we'd expect ~30 TWh/yr of residential electric consumption across ~3M Washington households (roughly 10 MWh/household/yr, near the national residential average):

```{python}
#| label: sanity-annual-total

annual_state_kwh = float(hourly_by_sub["kwh_w_state"].sum())
hourly_state = (
    hourly_by_sub.group_by("timestamp")
    .agg(pl.sum("kwh_w_state").alias("kwh_w_total"))
    .sort("timestamp")
)
print(f"Total annual residential kWh: {annual_state_kwh:,.0f}")
print(f"Total annual residential GWh: {annual_state_kwh / 1e6:,.0f}")
print(
    f"Per-household annual kWh: "
    f"{annual_state_kwh / float(md['weight'].sum()):,.0f}"
)
print(
    f"Statewide hourly load — mean: {float(hourly_state['kwh_w_total'].mean()) / 1e3:,.1f} MWh/h, "
    f"max: {float(hourly_state['kwh_w_total'].max()) / 1e3:,.1f} MWh/h"
)
```

## Identify the top 100 system-load hours

The "system" here is residential-only: the sum of weighted ResStock household loads. Washington is a winter-peaking system, so we expect these top hours to cluster in cold-snap evenings (Dec–Feb, 6–8 PM).

```{python}
#| label: top-hours

top_hours = (
    hourly_state.sort("kwh_w_total", descending=True)
    .head(TOP_N_HOURS)
    .select("timestamp", "kwh_w_total")
)
top_hours.head(5)
```

```{python}
#| label: top-hours-distribution

top_hours_dist = (
    top_hours.with_columns(
        pl.col("timestamp").dt.month().alias("month"),
        pl.col("timestamp").dt.hour().alias("hour"),
    )
    .group_by("month")
    .agg(pl.len().alias("n_hours"))
    .sort("month")
)
print("Top-100 hour count by month:")
print(top_hours_dist)

print()
print("Top-100 hour count by hour-of-day:")
print(
    top_hours.with_columns(pl.col("timestamp").dt.hour().alias("hour"))
    .group_by("hour")
    .agg(pl.len().alias("n_hours"))
    .sort("n_hours", descending=True)
    .head(8)
)
```

## Compute the three percentages

We're computing three numbers per heating group:

- `pct_customers` — share of weighted households (revenue proxy at the customer-charge level)
- `pct_annual_kwh` — share of total residential annual kWh (revenue proxy under a flat volumetric rate)
- `pct_top100_kwh` — share of residential kWh consumed during the top 100 system-load hours (cost-of-service / demand-allocator proxy)

```{python}
#| label: subclass-shares

# Share of customers, by subclass.
pct_customers = (
    md.group_by("subclass")
    .agg(pl.col("weight").sum().alias("n_customers"))
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("n_customers") / pl.col("n_customers").sum()).alias("pct_customers"),
    )
    .select("subclass", "pct_customers", "n_customers")
)

# Share of total annual consumption, by subclass.
pct_annual_kwh = (
    hourly_by_sub.group_by("subclass")
    .agg(pl.col("kwh_w_state").sum().alias("annual_kwh"))
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("annual_kwh") / pl.col("annual_kwh").sum()).alias("pct_annual_kwh"),
    )
    .select("subclass", "pct_annual_kwh", "annual_kwh")
)

# Share of load in the top 100 hours, by subclass.
top_set = top_hours.select("timestamp")
pct_top100_kwh = (
    hourly_by_sub.join(top_set, on="timestamp", how="inner")
    .group_by("subclass")
    .agg(pl.col("kwh_w_state").sum().alias("top100_kwh"))
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("top100_kwh") / pl.col("top100_kwh").sum()).alias("pct_top100_kwh"),
    )
    .select("subclass", "pct_top100_kwh", "top100_kwh")
)
```

Assemble into one tidy frame with a fixed row order (HP, ER, NG, delivered fuels, other) plus an "All customers" total row at the bottom:

```{python}
#| label: assemble-table

spine = pl.DataFrame({"subclass": list(HT_V2_ORDER)})

shares = (
    spine
    .join(pct_customers, on="subclass", how="left")
    .join(pct_annual_kwh, on="subclass", how="left")
    .join(pct_top100_kwh, on="subclass", how="left")
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("pct_customers").fill_null(0.0),
        pl.col("pct_annual_kwh").fill_null(0.0),
        pl.col("pct_top100_kwh").fill_null(0.0),
    )
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("subclass").replace(HT_V2_LABELS).alias("subclass"),
    )
    .select("subclass", "pct_customers", "pct_annual_kwh", "pct_top100_kwh")
)

total_row = pl.DataFrame(
    {
        "subclass": ["All customers"],
        "pct_customers": [float(shares["pct_customers"].sum())],
        "pct_annual_kwh": [float(shares["pct_annual_kwh"].sum())],
        "pct_top100_kwh": [float(shares["pct_top100_kwh"].sum())],
    }
)

shares_tbl = pl.concat([shares, total_row], how="vertical")
shares_tbl
```

## The table

```{python}
#| label: tbl-wa-heating-group-share
#| tbl-cap: "Washington residential customers — share of customers, share of annual electric consumption, and share of load in the top 100 system-load hours, by heating group."

_gt = (
    GT(shares_tbl, rowname_col="subclass")
    .tab_header(
        title=(
            "Washington residential heating groups: share of customers, "
            "energy, and peak load"
        ),
        subtitle=(
            "Heat pump and electric-resistance customers' share of load "
            "in the top 100 system-load hours, compared to their share of "
            "customers and annual consumption"
        ),
    )
    .tab_stubhead(label="Heating group")
    .cols_label(
        pct_customers="% of customers",
        pct_annual_kwh="% of annual consumption",
        pct_top100_kwh="% of load in top 100 hours",
    )
    .fmt_percent(
        columns=["pct_customers", "pct_annual_kwh", "pct_top100_kwh"],
        decimals=1,
    )
    .sub_missing(missing_text="—")
    .tab_options(**get_switchbox_gt_tab_options())
    .cols_align(
        align="center",
        columns=["pct_customers", "pct_annual_kwh", "pct_top100_kwh"],
    )
    .tab_style(style=style.text(align="center"), locations=loc.column_labels())
    .tab_source_note(
        "ResStock 2024 (res_2024_amy2018_2_sb), Washington, upgrade 00 "
        "(baseline). Heating groups derived from "
        "postprocess_group.heating_type_v2. Top 100 hours selected from "
        "statewide weighted residential hourly load."
    )
)
display_gt(_gt)
```

## Quick interpretation

Compare the **% of annual consumption** column (revenue proxy under a flat volumetric rate) to the **% of load in top 100 hours** column (cost-of-service proxy via a demand allocator):

- A heating group whose top-100-hour share **exceeds** its annual-consumption share is a **net cost-driver** under flat volumetric rates — they impose more system cost than they pay for through energy charges.
- A group whose top-100-hour share is **below** its annual-consumption share is a **net cost-payer** — they subsidize the cost-drivers.

Compare the **% of customers** column to the same two: customers paying high fixed/customer charges contribute disproportionately at that flat-charge level.
